Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I tried to set aside time in the evenings after work, but being 29 and juggling everything, it rarely worked out the way I planned. I'd sit down with my notes and readings, but my mind would wander to bills, to whether I was even cut out for this. Some weeks I'd manage to put in solid hours, other weeks I barely touched anything. The sadness just made it hard to stick to any kind of schedule.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how overwhelmed I felt. When deadlines loomed, I'd panic and try to cram everything in at once, which only made my anxiety worse. Other times, when nothing was immediately pressing, I'd drift away from studying entirely. I wanted to be consistent, but life kept getting in the way and I couldn't find my footing. The stress of being behind all the time made it even harder to establish any regularity.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I remembered an assignment was due or when I got a notification reminder. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which I often had. The guilt of falling behind would push me to open it up, even if I wasn't ready to actually do the work. It wasn't ever a pleasant feeling logging in—more like facing something I'd been avoiding.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a comfortable routine for me. There were brief periods where I'd check in more regularly, especially when the gamified tasks were introduced because they felt slightly less intimidating. But that didn't last long. My anxiety would spike every time I saw how much I hadn't completed, so I'd close the browser and tell myself I'd come back later. It never really stuck as a habit.
Q5: The discussion posts were actually helpful because I could read what other students were thinking and feel less alone in my confusion. I also appreciated the reading materials when I could print them out and study them away from a screen. The gamified tasks they introduced were okay—they broke things into smaller pieces which felt more manageable for someone like me who gets overwhelmed easily. Those were the moments I felt like maybe I could do this.
Q6: I often ignored the videos because without reliable internet at home, streaming was a nightmare. I'd start watching and it would buffer endlessly, which just frustrated me more. Some of the optional readings I skipped entirely because I was already drowning in the required stuff. If something seemed too long or complicated, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, but later never came. My low resources made accessing certain things feel impossible.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to passing the course or if it didn't take forever to complete. The gamified tasks felt worthwhile because they gave me small wins, which I desperately needed. If something was going to take hours and I couldn't see the payoff, my motivation just evaporated. I needed things that didn't make me feel more behind than I already was.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're struggling to complete even the required work, optional stuff just adds to the guilt pile. Required assignments had this weight of consequence that pushed me to at least attempt them. Optional things, no matter how helpful they might be, always got pushed aside because I was in survival mode. It's not that I didn't want to learn more—I just couldn't.
Q9: Yes, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd print out readings at the library since I don't have reliable internet at home, and I'd work through them with a pen and notebook. I also used old textbooks I borrowed and made handwritten notes that I'd review on the bus or during breaks. None of that showed up anywhere in the system, but it was real studying. Sometimes the most productive learning happened completely disconnected from any technology.
Q10: There were many times I sat with printed materials for hours, really trying to understand concepts, but the LMS wouldn't know any of that. I'd reread my handwritten notes, quiz myself, talk through ideas out loud—none of which generates any data. Some of my best study sessions happened at the kitchen table with no computer in sight. The system only sees clicks, not comprehension or effort.
Q11: Absolutely. I'd download a PDF or print something once, and then that material would be my companion for days or weeks. I couldn't afford to keep going back online to access things repeatedly. So one download might represent hours of subsequent offline study. The LMS would show one access, but that single download could mean a dozen study sessions with that same document.
Q12: There were definitely moments when everything felt impossible. When my anxiety was at its worst, I couldn't even look at the course materials without feeling sick. Financial stress would pile up, and I'd have to pick up extra shifts instead of studying. The sadness would hit and I'd lose whole days just trying to function. Those were the times when engagement dropped to almost nothing, even though I desperately wanted to keep up.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. The workload felt crushing when combined with my job and personal responsibilities. Confusion about what was expected made me freeze up instead of asking for help. Fatigue from working and studying left me unable to concentrate even when I had time. Everything competed for my limited energy, and the course often lost that competition.
Q14: Many times I'd open materials and just stare at them without anything sinking in. My eyes would move across the words but my mind was somewhere else—worrying about money, feeling inadequate, spiraling into anxiety. I'd have the LMS open for an hour and absorb almost nothing. Those sessions looked like engagement in the data, but they were really just me going through the motions while drowning internally.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, even when I knew it would hurt me. The anxiety made starting anything feel terrifying—like if I tried and failed, it would confirm I wasn't smart enough. So I'd avoid and avoid until the deadline was right there. It wasn't laziness; it was fear. Every postponement made the next attempt feel even more impossible, and the cycle just kept repeating.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that really forced me to act. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. But they also caused massive stress spikes because I'd leave everything until the last minute. I'd be up late, panicking, trying to submit something—anything. The quality of my work suffered because of this pattern, but I couldn't seem to break it no matter how much I wanted to.
Q17: Feedback was a double-edged sword for me. When it was encouraging, I'd feel a brief spark of hope. But often I'd avoid looking at feedback because I was terrified of what it might say. Negative feedback would send me into a spiral that made it even harder to engage with the next assignment. I know feedback is supposed to help, but with my anxiety, it sometimes felt like another source of pain.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with 19 online courses to manage. I never felt like I had a clear path through everything—it was just this massive wall of requirements. When they introduced the gamified elements, that helped a little because it broke things down. But overall, the structure didn't account for someone like me who needs more guidance and smaller steps. I often felt lost in the sheer volume of it all.
Q19: Having so many courses made engagement incredibly difficult. The lack of clear prioritization left me paralyzed about where to start. The gamified tasks were a design choice that actually helped—they made things feel achievable. But not having reliable internet access meant many design features just didn't work for me. The course assumed a level of access and stability that I simply didn't have.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well sometimes. I'd be clicking around, opening things, trying to figure out what to do, but never actually completing the work. High activity can mean someone is lost and searching, not necessarily learning. It can also mean someone is anxious and checking obsessively without making progress. The activity looks productive, but inside it might be chaos.
Q21: That wasn't really my experience since I struggled on both fronts. But I can imagine someone who studies efficiently offline and only logs in to submit might look inactive but actually be doing well. The LMS can't see what happens away from the screen. Some people just don't need to click around constantly to learn—they're more focused and deliberate than I was able to be.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click, how long they spend. But they miss everything that matters emotionally and contextually. They can't see that I was crying while staring at an assignment, or that I studied for hours with printed notes. They show the skeleton of engagement but miss the flesh and blood of what learning actually feels like for each person.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling like you're part of something. It means your mind is present, not just your cursor. For me, true engagement happened in rare moments when the material clicked and I forgot my anxiety for a while. It's not about time spent or clicks made—it's about connection and growth. I wanted that feeling so badly, but I couldn't hold onto it.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how hard I was trying despite everything working against me. It wouldn't show the tears, the self-doubt, the hours spent offline with printed materials. It wouldn't capture that I genuinely wanted to succeed but was fighting my own mind every step of the way. My engagement looked low in the system, but the effort and emotional investment were actually enormous—they just didn't translate into results or data points.
